claimed_contribution: Introduces a Bayesian dual-objective compound design criterion (the f-criterion) for selective choice processes, jointly targeting efficient estimation of active attributes and detection of effects among attributes presumed inactive.

Develops an integrated framework that uses self-stated active/inactive auxiliary data in two places: respondent-level customized conjoint design construction and estimation of a heterogeneous variable selection model.

Demonstrates practical feasibility of instantaneous individual design customization and documents performance gains versus standard D-criterion benchmarks through simulations and an empirical conjoint study.
context: The paper studies conjoint choice experiments in which consumers may base decisions on only a subset of attributes, so attribute activity can vary across people. The empirical context is an online U.S. tablet computer conjoint study with eight binary attributes.
design_and_data: The authors derive a compound Bayesian design criterion and benchmark f-designs against three Bayesian D-design variants (D0, DI, DS) in an 8-attribute, 18-choice-set, 2-alternative conjoint setting across seven variable-selection scenarios and multiple prior/data-generating settings. They compute relative design efficiency metrics using repeated draws from assumed true parameter distributions and examine robustness to sparsity misspecification and criterion-weight choices. They run a simulation with N = 400 pseudo-individuals (100 replications) comparing no-customization D0 versus customized f-designs, with holdout tasks for predictive validation. They also conduct a randomized online experiment (488 completes; 468 after quality checks) comparing a no-customization D-design group (N = 233) and a customized f-design group (N = 235), estimate a heterogeneous variable selection model with auxiliary data (plus a random-effects logit benchmark), and evaluate in-sample fit and holdout prediction.
main_findings: In concordant variable-selection settings, f-designs were more efficient on average than D-designs for estimating all parameters (about 30.2% vs D0, 3.7% vs DI, and 6.1% vs DS), with larger gains for estimating active-attribute effects.

Across variance settings, f-designs outperformed D0 and DI in all tested settings and outperformed DS in most settings, and their advantages generally persisted under moderate sparsity misspecification.

In both simulation and the empirical tablet conjoint experiment, customized f-designs improved recovery and prediction versus no-customization D-designs (including higher holdout hit rates), and they yielded materially different managerial inferences such as attribute trade-offs and predicted choice shares.
one_line_summary: The paper proposes a self-stated-data-driven compound f-criterion for customizing conjoint designs under selective choice and shows it improves efficiency and predictive performance relative to standard D-design approaches.
relation_to_prior_work: introduces the compound design criterion into marketing choice-design research, which has mainly emphasized single-objective Bayesian D-criteria, and extends selective-choice modeling by integrating auxiliary active/inactive self-statements into both design and estimation.
research_gap: The authors state that two integrated issues have received scant attention: efficient design of choice experiments under selective choice and analysis of data generated by such processes. Existing efficient choice design work in marketing largely focuses on a single objective (estimating all parameters) rather than jointly emphasizing active-attribute estimation and detection of potentially active attributes labeled inactive.
research_question: How should choice experiments be designed and analyzed when respondents use variable selection and ignore some attributes? Specifically, can self-stated active/inactive attribute information be leveraged to construct respondent-level customized designs that both estimate active-attribute effects efficiently and detect mislabeled inactive effects, while improving inference and market prediction compared with standard D-criterion approaches?
